title: 4-7-8 Breathing
description: A calming breathing pattern to settle stress and support sleep.
steps: Sit comfortably and relax your shoulders.
Inhale through your nose for 4 seconds.
Hold your breath for 7 seconds.
Exhale slowly through your mouth for 8 seconds.
Repeat for 4–8 rounds.
tips: If 4–7–8 feels intense, shorten counts (e.g., 3–5–6) and keep the exhale longest.

title: 5-4-3-2-1 Grounding
description: A quick grounding practice to reduce anxiety by engaging the senses.
steps: Name 5 things you can see.
Name 4 things you can feel (touch).
Name 3 things you can hear.
Name 2 things you can smell.
Name 1 thing you can taste or like about this moment.
tips: Say each item out loud if possible—hearing your voice can make it more grounding.

title: Thought Challenging
description: Reframe an unhelpful thought by testing it like a hypothesis.
steps: Write the thought exactly as it appears in your mind.
Label the feeling and rate intensity (0–10).
List evidence that supports the thought.
List evidence that does not support the thought.
Write a more balanced alternative thought.
Re-rate the feeling (0–10).
tips: Aim for ‘balanced and believable,’ not ‘perfectly positive.’

title: Gratitude Journaling
description: A short journaling prompt to shift attention toward what’s working.
steps: Write 3 specific things you appreciate today.
For each, add one sentence about why it mattered.
If you can, note one tiny action that helped it happen.
tips: Specific beats big: ‘hot tea after work’ is more effective than ‘my life.’

title: Progressive Muscle Relaxation
description: Release physical tension by tensing and relaxing muscle groups from head to toe.
steps: Start with 2 slow breaths.
Tense your feet for 5 seconds, then relax for 10 seconds.
Repeat for calves, thighs, glutes, abdomen, hands, arms, shoulders, jaw, and forehead.
Finish with 3 slow breaths and notice the difference.
tips: Tense firmly but never to pain; the contrast is what teaches your body to relax.

title: Box Breathing
description: A steady 4-part breath to calm the nervous system and improve focus.
steps: Inhale for 4 seconds.
Hold for 4 seconds.
Exhale for 4 seconds.
Hold for 4 seconds.
Repeat for 5 minutes.
tips: Keep the breath quiet and gentle; imagine tracing the edges of a box.

title: Pursed-Lip Breathing
description: Slow your exhale to reduce panic sensations and ease shortness of breath.
steps: Inhale through your nose for 2 seconds.
Purse your lips as if blowing through a straw.
Exhale slowly for 4–6 seconds.
Repeat for 3–5 minutes.
tips: Let the exhale be longer than the inhale—this is the calming lever.

title: Body Scan Meditation
description: A guided attention scan to reduce tension and reconnect with the body.
steps: Lie down or sit comfortably and close your eyes.
Bring attention to your feet; notice sensations without changing them.
Slowly move attention upward: legs → hips → abdomen → chest → shoulders → arms → hands.
Notice jaw, face, and forehead; soften any tightness.
End with 3 slow breaths.
tips: If your mind wanders, gently return to the last body area you remember.

title: Mindful Walking
description: Turn a short walk into a grounding practice using sensation and attention.
steps: Walk at a comfortable pace.
Notice contact: heel → midfoot → toes.
Feel the movement of your legs and arms.
Name 3 things you see, 2 you hear, 1 you feel each minute.
If thoughts arise, return attention to your steps.
tips: Try one ‘silent minute’ where you only notice sensations, no judgments.

title: Self-Compassion Break
description: A quick practice to respond to stress with kindness instead of criticism.
steps: Place a hand on your chest or cheek.
Say: ‘This is a tough moment.’
Say: ‘Tough moments are part of being human.’
Say: ‘May I be kind to myself right now.’
Choose one gentle next action (water, stretch, message a friend).
tips: Use your own words—what would you say to a friend in the same situation?

title: Positive Affirmations (Believable)
description: Create believable affirmations that reduce self-doubt and support action.
steps: Write one worry or self-criticism you notice today.
Rewrite it into a balanced statement you can believe.
Repeat the balanced statement 5 times slowly.
Pick one small action that aligns with it.
tips: Believable > perfect: ‘I’m learning’ works better than ‘I’m the best.’

title: Visualization: Safe Place
description: Use mental imagery to create calm and reduce stress arousal.
steps: Close your eyes and take 3 slow breaths.
Imagine a place that feels safe or peaceful.
Add details: light, colors, sounds, temperature, textures.
On each exhale, imagine releasing tension into the environment.
Open your eyes and name 3 things you notice around you.
tips: If imagery is hard, use a real memory (a park, a room, a beach photo).

title: Scheduled Worry Time
description: Contain worry by giving it a scheduled slot instead of letting it spread all day.
steps: Pick a daily 10-minute time window for worrying (same time each day).
When worry shows up earlier, write it down and say ‘Later.’
During worry time, read your list and worry on purpose.
Finish by writing one next-step for any item that has an action.
tips: If you start worrying outside the window, redirect to the note—practice is the point.

title: Thought Labeling
description: Reduce overthinking by labeling thoughts as mental events (not facts).
steps: Sit and breathe naturally for 30 seconds.
When a thought appears, label it: ‘planning’, ‘worrying’, ‘judging’, ‘remembering’.
Return to the breath after each label.
Continue for 5 minutes.
tips: Label gently; you’re not trying to stop thoughts—just change your relationship to them.

title: Stress Release Writing
description: Unload stress onto paper to reduce mental clutter and improve clarity.
steps: Set a timer for 10 minutes.
Write continuously about what’s stressing you—no editing.
If you get stuck, write ‘I feel…’ and continue.
At the end, circle one sentence that feels most important.
tips: You can destroy the page after—privacy often makes writing easier.

title: Values Check-In
description: Reconnect with what matters to guide your next small step.
steps: Write 3 values that matter to you (e.g., growth, family, health).
Pick one value for today.
Write one small action you can do in the next 24 hours aligned with it.
Schedule it (time + place).
tips: Make the action tiny—values are practiced through small repeated moves.

title: Digital Detox Break
description: A short reset to reduce overstimulation from screens and notifications.
steps: Turn on Do Not Disturb for 10 minutes.
Put your phone face down or in another room.
Do one low-stimulation activity: stretch, tea, slow walk, or breathing.
Return and check notifications once, then close apps again.
tips: Try pairing this with a specific trigger: after lunch or before bed.

title: Mindful Sipping (Tea/Coffee/Water)
description: A micro-practice to slow down using taste and temperature.
steps: Take one slow sip.
Notice temperature and texture.
Notice taste and aftertaste.
Take 3 more sips with full attention.
tips: If distractions come up, return to one sensation (warmth, sweetness, breath).

title: Name the Feeling
description: Increase emotional clarity by labeling and locating the emotion in the body.
steps: Ask: ‘What am I feeling right now?’
Pick 1–2 labels (e.g., anxious, sad, frustrated).
Rate intensity 0–10.
Locate where you feel it (chest, throat, stomach).
Take 5 slow breaths and re-rate intensity.
tips: If you can’t find a label, start with ‘pleasant / unpleasant / neutral’ and refine.
